draftParagraphs: Article 1. Scope of adjustment

This Decree stipulates in detail Clauses 2 and 3, Article 2; Clause 5, Article 4; Clause 3, Article 6; Clause 5, Article 9; Clause 3, Article 17; Clause 7, Article 20; Clause 7, Article 21; Clause 4, Article 22; Clause 5, Article 23; Clause 3, Article 27; Clause 6, Article 30; Point b, Clause 4, Article 31; Clause 3, Article 33; Article 35; Clause 4, Article 38 of the Law on Personal Data Protection and stipulates measures to organize the implementation of the Law on research and development of personal data protection solutions, specialized agencies for personal data protection, National Information Portal on personal data protection; Responsibilities of ministries, branches and localities in protecting personal data; Funding to ensure personal data protection activities.
Article 2. Subjects of application
1. Vietnamese agencies, organizations and individuals;
2. Foreign agencies, organizations and individuals in Vietnam;

3. Foreign agencies, organizations, and individuals directly participating in or related to the processing of personal data of Vietnamese citizens and people of Vietnamese origin of unknown nationality living in Vietnam who have been issued identification certificates.
Article 3. Basic personal data categories
Basic personal data includes:
1. Surname, middle name, birth name, other names (if any);
2. Date, month, year of birth; date, month, year of death or disappearance;
3. Gender;
4. Place of birth, place of birth registration, place of permanent residence, place of temporary residence, current residence, hometown, contact address;
5. Nationality;
6. Image of the individual;
7. Phone number, personal identification number, passport number, driver's license number, license plate number;
8. Marital status;
9. Information about family relationships (parents, children, wife, husband);
10. Information about individual digital accounts;
11. Other information associated with a specific person or helping to identify a specific person is not regulated in Article 4 of this Decree.
Article 4. Categories of sensitive personal data
1. Sensitive personal data includes:
a) Data revealing racial or ethnic origin;
b) Viewpoints on politics, religion, beliefs;
c) Information about private life, personal secrets, family secrets;
d) Health status;
d) Biometric data, genetic characteristics;
e) Data revealing the individual's sex life and sexual orientation;
g) Data on crimes and law violations collected and stored by law enforcement agencies;
h) The individual's location is determined through location services;
i) Login name and password information to access the individual's electronic identity account; image of identification card, citizen identification card, identity card;

k) Username and access password of bank account; Bank card information, transaction history data of bank accounts; financial and credit information and information about activities and history of financial, securities and insurance transactions of customers at credit institutions, foreign bank branches, payment intermediary service providers, securities, insurance, and other permitted organizations;
l) Data tracking behavior and activities using telecommunications services, social networks, online media services and other services in cyberspace;
m) Other personal data required by law to be kept confidential or require strict security measures.
2. In the process of processing sensitive personal data, agencies and organizations must establish decentralization regulations that limit access, processing procedures and security measures.
Article 5. Exercise of rights of personal data subjects

1. The personal data controller and the party that controls and processes personal data must develop clear processes, procedures and forms to exercise the rights of personal data subjects in accordance with personal data processing activities and responsibilities of relevant departments; ensure that personal data subjects are informed about the procedures for exercising the rights specified in Clause 1, Article 4 of the Law on Personal Data Protection.

2. When receiving a request to withdraw consent to process personal data, limit processing of personal data, object to processing personal data in accordance with the procedures of the personal data subject, the personal data controller, the controller and processor of personal data must respond within 02 working days, provide full information to the personal data subject about the procedure to stop processing personal data and do so within 15 days, except In cases where personal data processing does not require the consent of the personal data subject according to the provisions of Article 19 of the Law on Personal Data Protection. In cases where it is necessary to request the personal data processor or third party to stop processing the personal data of the personal data subject, this shall be done within 20 days.

Depending on the nature and complexity of the request, in case it is necessary to extend the processing time, it will be extended by a maximum of 01 extension within a period of no more than 15 days. The personal data controller, personal data controller and processor shall notify the personal data subject of the reason for the extension and is responsible for proving that the extension is necessary and reasonable.

3. When receiving a request to view, edit or request to edit personal data, provide personal data in accordance with the personal data subject's procedures, the personal data controller, the personal data controller and processor must respond within 02 working days, provide full information to the personal data subject about the procedures and implementation within 10 days. In case it is necessary to request the personal data processor or third party to edit the personal data of the personal data subject, this must be done within 15 days.

Depending on the nature and complexity of the request, in case it is necessary to extend the processing time, it will be extended by a maximum of 01 extension within a period of no more than 10 days. The personal data controller, personal data controller and processor shall notify the personal data subject of the reason for the extension and is responsible for proving that the extension is necessary and reasonable.

4. When receiving a request to delete personal data in accordance with the procedures of the personal data subject, the personal data controller, the party that controls and processes personal data must respond within 02 working days, provide full information to the personal data subject about the procedure and implementation within 20 days. In case it is necessary to request the personal data processor or third party to provide, delete, or limit the processing of the personal data subject's data, this shall be done within 30 days.

Depending on the nature and complexity of the request, in case it is necessary to extend the processing time, a maximum of 01 extension can be extended within a period of no more than 20 days. The personal data controller, personal data controller and processor shall notify the personal data subject of the reason for the extension and is responsible for proving that the extension is necessary and reasonable.

5. When receiving a request to implement measures and solutions to protect your personal data in accordance with the procedures of the personal data subject, the competent authority or agency, organization or individual involved in processing personal data must respond within 02 working days, provide full information to the personal data subject about the procedures and implementation within 15 days.

Depending on the nature and complexity of the request, in case it is necessary to extend the processing time, it will be extended by a maximum of 01 extension within a period of no more than 15 days. The personal data controller, personal data controller and processor shall notify the personal data subject of the reason for the extension and is responsible for proving that the extension is necessary and reasonable.
Article 6. Method of expressing consent of the personal data subject
1. Methods of obtaining consent from personal data subjects must ensure verifiability of identifying the personal data subject who has provided consent, the time and content of consent, including:
a) In writing;
b) By recorded call;
c) Syntax for consent via phone message;
d) Via email, on electronic information pages, platforms, applications with technical setup, ask for consent;
d) By other suitable methods that can be printed or copied in writing, including in electronic or verifiable format.

2. The data controller, the party that controls and processes personal data, must store the consent of the data subject. In the event of a dispute, the burden of proving the consent of the data subject lies with the personal data controller, the party that controls and processes the personal data.

3. The personal data controller, the party that controls and processes personal data, must not establish a default method of consent or create unclear instructions, causing misunderstanding between consent and disagreement for data subjects. Available default settings must ensure personal data protection principles, respecting the rights of personal data subjects.
4. For obtaining consent to process sensitive personal data, the data subject must be informed that the data to be processed is sensitive personal data.
Article 7. Transfer of personal data

1. Organizations and individuals transferring personal data according to Points a, c, d, Clause 1, Article 17 of the Law on Personal Data Protection must establish an agreement on the transfer of personal data with the recipient of personal data, clearly stating the following contents:
a) Purpose of transferring personal data;
b) The subject of personal data and the type of personal data transferred are appropriate to the purpose of transfer;
c) Time limit for processing personal data, requests for deletion and destruction of personal data after completing the purpose of transfer;
d) Legal basis of personal data transfer;
d) Responsibility to protect personal data during the transfer and processing of personal data;
e) Responsibility for exercising the rights of personal data subjects;
g) Responsibility for coordination and compliance of the parties in case of detection of violations of personal data protection regulations.

2. The transfer of sensitive personal data must have physical security measures for storage and transmission equipment, encryption measures, anonymization of personal data and other security measures during the transfer process.

3. In case of transferring personal data according to Points a and d, Clause 1, Article 17 of the Law on Personal Data Protection for a fee to provide services to personal data subjects or to serve the legitimate interests of personal data subjects, organizations and individuals must comply with the following regulations:

a) Must establish a technical system and transparent mechanism for personal data subjects to accurately and clearly consent to each transfer, on the basis of knowing the exact purpose of transfer, organizations and individuals receiving and processing personal data;
b) Only process personal data for the purpose of transfer agreed by the personal data subject, in accordance with the registered business industry and profession;
c) The type of personal data transferred must be limited to the purpose of the transfer;
d) Do not collect, store, or form a personal data warehouse from the transfer of personal data to use for purposes other than those agreed by the personal data subject;
d) Clearly define the roles of personal data controllers, personal data processors, and third parties in personal data transfer activities;
e) There must be an agreement to transfer and process personal data before transferring and commitment to responsibilities and obligations to the personal data subject.

4. In case of sharing personal data between departments within the same agency or organization to process personal data in accordance with the established processing purpose, the agency or organization must develop a process to control the sharing and use of personal data in accordance with regulations; Take measures to prevent internal personnel of agencies and organizations from illegally sharing personal data with third parties.
5. Personal data must be de-identified before trading on the data exchange.

6. Agencies, organizations and individuals that provide personal data according to Clause 2, Article 15 of the Law on Personal Data Protection based on each specific request of the data subject are not considered data transfers and are not required to comply with the provisions of this Article.
Article 8. Protection of personal data in financial, banking and credit information activities

1. Organizations and individuals operating in the fields of finance, banking, and credit information activities are responsible for applying standards and technical regulations to protect personal data; Technical regulations on de-identification of personal data and anonymization of personal data issued and applied in Vietnam; Conduct periodic assessments of compliance with regulations on personal data protection once a year; keep a log of all personal data processing activities.

2. Organizations and individuals operating in the fields of finance, banking, and credit information activities that are the controllers of personal data, and the parties that control and process personal data when asking for consent from the subject of personal data must ensure to clearly state:
a) Personal data processing purposes, including scoring, credit rating, credit information assessment, credit credit assessment if any;
b) Sources of personal data collection and relevant parties collecting and sharing personal data;
c) Personal data storage period;
d) Mechanism and method of withdrawing consent and policy on deletion and destruction of personal data according to regulations.

3. Within no more than 72 hours after discovering the exposure or loss of sensitive data of personal data subjects in the fields of finance, banking, and credit information activities, organizations and individuals directly collecting personal data of data subjects are responsible for notifying the agency in charge of protecting personal data and the personal data subject. The content of the notification must ensure at least the contents specified in Clause 1, Article 28 of this Decree.
Article 9. Protection of personal data in big data processing

1. Processing big data containing personal data is the activity of processing personal data on a large scale, continuously, integrating from many different sources, capable of analyzing behavior, predicting trends or classifying users.
2. In case of processing big data containing personal data, relevant agencies, organizations and individuals are responsible for:
a) Comply with regulations on personal data protection during data processing from the moment processing begins;
b) Only collect, process and store personal data in the right scope and in accordance with specific and clear purposes;
c) Develop appropriate policies for storing, deleting, and destroying personal data in accordance with the law;

d) Organize periodic training, dissemination and awareness raising on personal data security and personal data protection measures for employees, especially personnel directly handling personal data. Increase awareness of the importance of personal data protection within the organization;
d) Have agreements with third parties, partners and service providers to ensure full compliance with regulations on personal data protection;
e) There is an appropriate notification and explanation mechanism for data subjects about their personal data being used in the big data analysis system.
3. Agencies, organizations and individuals apply personal data protection measures during the processing of big data, including:
a) Apply measures to ensure network security, data confidentiality, and prevent personal data loss during the process of storing, processing, and transmitting personal data;

b) Use a strong authentication method, requiring at least multi-factor authentication (password, PIN combined with one-time password, digital signing device or biometric factor), appropriate to the sensitivity level of personal data; Decentralize access to ensure only authorized people can access personal data;

c) Implement encryption and anonymization of personal data (the process of separating data that identifies a specific person for separate storage and security; personal data after this process is used for processing without being able to identify a specific person) during the process of transferring and providing personal data, unless otherwise prescribed by specialized laws or when processing requires data in plaintext form to serve crime prevention, anti-money laundering, and security assurance. country, handling customer complaints and disputes. In these cases, agencies and organizations must apply additional security solutions to ensure personal data is not accessed or used illegally;
d) Carry out continuous monitoring, using monitoring tools to monitor personal data access activities and detect unusual behavior;
d) Carry out periodic inspection and assessment of network security and data security to detect, prevent and fix security holes.
Article 10. Protection of personal data in artificial intelligence systems and virtual universes

1. Organizations and individuals have the right to use personal data to research and develop self-learning algorithms, artificial intelligence systems and other automatic systems but need to ensure compliance with regulations on personal data protection.
2. If data from artificial intelligence inference results can be used to identify or help identify a specific person, personal data protection measures must be applied in accordance with the law.

3. The personal data controller, the party that controls and processes personal data, is responsible for informing the data subject about the automatic processing of personal data, explaining the operating principles of the algorithm and the impact on the data subject's legitimate rights and interests; provide options for data subjects to opt out.

4. A metaverse is a digital universe that combines aspects of social media, online gaming, augmented reality (AR), virtual reality (VR), the Internet, and cryptocurrency to allow users to use virtual reality technology to interact.
5. Organizations and individuals apply personal data protection measures in artificial intelligence systems and virtual universes, including:

a) Research, build and deploy a system that meets cybersecurity standards and comprehensive data protection standards for artificial intelligence systems, especially focusing on factors: information security, algorithm reliability, system stability and ability to prevent cyber attacks;

b) Establish a mechanism to monitor the operation of the artificial intelligence system in two aspects: supervision by competent state agencies; Accountability towards personal data subjects of personal data controllers, controllers and processors of personal data.
c) Develop a mechanism to protect personal data according to appropriate standards, develop a monitoring system and early warning of cybersecurity risks;
d) Establish a control mechanism and prevent the abuse of artificial intelligence and virtual space in activities that violate national security, social order and safety;
d) Conduct periodic assessments of compliance with regulations on personal data protection once a year.
6. Personal data subjects must have the right to edit, anonymize, and delete identification records, including when the platform stores behavioral history.
Article 11. Protection of personal data in blockchain technology

1. Relevant agencies, organizations and individuals when processing personal data in blockchain technology are responsible for complying with regulations on personal data protection during the process of researching and developing products, services, applications, systems using blockchain technology and in the process of processing personal data.
2. Agencies, organizations and individuals apply personal data protection measures when processing personal data in blockchain technology, including:
a) Only apply encryption algorithms, hash algorithms, and digital signing algorithms to ensure safety;
b) Do not store personal data directly on the blockchain, only store when personal data has been de-identified or store hash values ​​of personal data;
c) Conduct periodic assessments of compliance with regulations on personal data protection once a year.
Article 12. Protection of personal data in cloud computing
1. Relevant agencies, organizations and individuals must apply technical and organizational measures to prevent personal data from being accessed illegally when deploying cloud computing services.
2. Organizations and individuals signing contracts related to personal data processing with organizations providing cloud computing services have the following responsibilities:

a) Clearly state in the contract content the compliance with Vietnamese law on personal data protection; Provide information about personal data protection departments and personnel; comply with regulations on administrative procedures related to personal data protection according to the provisions of law;
b) Clearly define the personal data processing flow, the roles of parties in the provision of cloud computing services and their respective responsibilities;
c) There are requirements for security, technical and organizational measures and are clearly stated in the contract;
d) Immediately notify relevant parties of any changes that may affect personal data;
d) Comply with personal data processing deadlines and requests for deletion and destruction of personal data;
e) Ensuring the exercise of the rights of personal data subjects;
g) Implement all technical measures to ensure access to personal data is properly decentralized.
3. Organizations providing cloud computing services:

a) Comply with Vietnam's regulations on personal data protection, provide information about personal data protection departments and personnel, and comply with regulations on administrative procedures related to personal data protection according to the provisions of law;
b) Request subcontractors to implement regulations and obligations to protect personal data according to the provisions of law;
c) Apply technical and organizational measures appropriate to the scale and level of processing of your personal data;
d) Conduct periodic assessments of compliance with regulations on personal data protection once a year.
4. Personal data on cloud computing must be encrypted at rest and in transit, with strict access permissions.
Article 13. Conditions of personal data protection personnel and personal data protection departments in agencies and organizations

1. Appointment of personal data protection personnel or personal data protection department must be made in an official document of that agency or organization; shows the assignment, functions, tasks, powers and other requirements for personal data protection in that agency or organization.
2. Personal data protection personnel appointed by agencies or organizations must meet the following competency conditions:
a) Have a college degree or higher;

b) Have at least 02 years of work experience (from the time of graduation) related to one of the fields of law, information technology, network security, data security, risk management, compliance control, human resource management, and personnel organization;
c) Have been trained and fostered legal knowledge and professional skills on personal data protection.
3. In case an agency or organization establishes a personal data protection department, the personnel in the department must fully meet the capacity conditions specified in Clause 2 of this Article.
4. Agencies and organizations responsible for evaluating and selecting personnel to protect personal data.

5. Agencies and organizations that sign security responsibility agreements with personal data protection personnel can agree on cases of liability exemption when violations or damage to protected personal data occur.

6. The agency or organization that appoints personal data protection personnel or the personal data protection department is responsible for training and fostering knowledge and skills on personal data protection for personal data protection personnel.
Article 14. Duties of personal data protection department and personal data protection personnel in agencies and organizations
1. The personal data protection department has the following functions and tasks:
a) Organize the development of policies, procedures, regulations, and forms to comply with legal regulations on personal data protection;
b) Organize and implement the rights of personal data subjects;

c) Periodically evaluate the organization's compliance with the law on personal data protection by reporting on the level of fulfillment of obligations under the law to propose measures to improve the effectiveness of legal compliance, prevent and control risks in personal data processing activities;

d) Prepare dossiers to assess the impact of cross-border personal data transfer, dossiers to assess the impact of processing personal data, receive and report violations of personal data protection and fulfill other requests of competent authorities according to regulations;
d) Develop plans and deploy periodic training and refresher courses on personal data protection in agencies and organizations;
e) Organize and implement technical measures for personal data security, personal data protection standards and regulations, and emergency response plans for personal data protection incidents;
g) Research and propose decisions related to personal data protection.
2. Personal data protection personnel have the following duties:
a) Advise on the development of policies, procedures, regulations, and forms to comply with legal regulations on personal data protection;
b) Participate in implementing the rights of personal data subjects;

c) Participate in periodic activities to evaluate the status of compliance with laws on personal data protection and propose measures to improve the effectiveness of legal compliance, prevent and control risks;

d) Prepare dossiers to assess the impact of cross-border personal data transfer, dossiers to assess the impact of processing personal data, receive and report violations of personal data protection and fulfill other requests of competent authorities according to regulations;
d) Participate in training programs and courses on personal data protection;
e) Participate in implementing technical measures for personal data security, personal data protection standards and regulations, and emergency response plans for personal data protection incidents.
Article 15. Individuals providing personal data protection services

1. An individual providing personal data protection services is a person who meets the capacity conditions specified in Clause 2 of this Article and is hired by an agency or organization as a personal data protection personnel.
2. Individuals providing personal data protection services must meet the following competency conditions:
a) Have a college degree or higher;

b) Have at least 03 years of work experience (from the time of graduation) related to one of the fields of law, personal data processing, network security, data security, risk management, compliance control;
c) Have received in-depth training and fostering of legal knowledge and professional skills on personal data protection.

3. Agencies and organizations wishing to hire individuals to provide personal data protection services shall consider capacity conditions according to Clause 2 of this Article and enter into a contract to use personal data protection personnel; Disclose information about personal data protection personnel for personal data subjects and relevant parties to know.
4. Individuals providing personal data protection services are responsible for:
a) Perform services according to the scope and tasks in the contract and agreement;
b) Do not take advantage of the provision of services to commit illegal acts;
c) Delete and destroy personal data processed during the service provision process after completing the contract and according to legal regulations.
Article 16. Organization providing personal data protection services
1. Organizations providing personal data protection services:

a) Is an organization or enterprise with functions, tasks or professions, business fields in technology, legal or technology and legal consulting hired by an agency or organization to advise on compliance with personal data protection regulations and perform personal data protection tasks according to the agreement;
b) Have at least 03 personnel who meet the capacity conditions according to Clause 2, Article 15 of this Decree;
c) Provided products and services related to security, network security, information technology, standards assessment, and consulting on personal data protection.

2. Organizations providing personal data protection services must develop capacity profiles proving their ability to protect personal data and provide them to agencies and organizations that need to use the service. Records must show profession and business field; scale, scope, and experience in providing services; service provision policy; standards, qualifications, and human resources capacity; Relevant documents and supporting documents.

3. Agencies and organizations wishing to hire personal data protection services shall review capacity profiles, sign service contracts and personal data processing agreements with personal data protection organizations; disclose information about that personal data protection organization to the personal data subject and relevant parties.

4. Agencies and organizations, depending on their needs, can simultaneously appoint personal data protection personnel, personal data protection departments, and hire individuals and organizations to provide personal data protection services.

5. Based on the agreement with the agency or organization that hires the personal data protection service, the organization providing the personal data protection service shall perform the tasks of the personal data protection department for that agency or organization.
6. Organizations providing personal data protection services are responsible for:
a) Perform services according to the scope and tasks in the contract and agreement;
b) Do not take advantage of the provision of services to commit illegal acts;
c) Delete and destroy personal data processed during the service provision process after completing the contract and according to legal regulations.
Article 17. Cross-border transfer of personal data
1. Personal data controllers, personal data controllers and processors, personal data processors and third parties carry out cross-border transfers of personal data in the following cases:

a) Personal data storage activities include the transfer of personal data collected and stored in Vietnam to a server system located outside the territory of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam or on cloud computing services of service providers abroad;
b) Activities of transferring personal data from agencies, organizations, and individuals from Vietnam to recipients who are organizations and individuals abroad;
c) Processing activities of personal data collected in Vietnam and transferred to platforms outside the territory of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam for further processing.
2. The agency in charge of personal data protection decides to request the cross-border data transfer party to stop cross-border transfer of personal data in the following cases:
a) When it is discovered that the transferred personal data is used for activities that violate national defense and security;
b) Committing violations of regulations on personal data protection that may harm national defense and security.

3. Cases other than the cases specified in Points a, b, c, Clause 6, Article 20 of the Law on Personal Data Protection that do not require implementation of regulations on impact assessment of cross-border transfer of personal data include:
a) Press and communication activities according to the provisions of law;
b) Cross-border transfers of personal data have been made public in accordance with the law;

c) In emergency situations, it is absolutely necessary to provide cross-border personal data to protect the life, health and property safety of individuals; to perform tasks and obligations according to the provisions of law;
d) Cross-border transfer of personal data for cross-border personnel management in accordance with labor rules, regulations and collective labor agreements as prescribed by law;

d) Providing cross-border personal data to sign contracts or carry out procedures related to cross-border transportation, logistics, money transfer, payment, hotels, visa applications, scholarship applications.
Article 18. Conditions, order, procedures, and components of documents assessing the impact of cross-border personal data transfer

1. Agencies, organizations and individuals that carry out cross-border personal data transfer activities according to the provisions of Clause 1, Article 20 of the Law on Personal Data Protection must prepare a dossier to assess the impact of cross-border personal data transfer according to the provisions of this Article.
2. Records assessing the impact of cross-border personal data transfers include:
a) Report on impact assessment of cross-border personal data transfer according to Form No. 09 in the Appendix to this Decree;
b) Copy of contract or personal data transfer document showing the binding and responsibilities between organizations and individuals transferring and receiving personal data across borders;
c) Policies, procedures, regulations, forms and other relevant documents on personal data protection of agencies, organizations and individuals with cross-border personal data transfer activities.
3. Report assessing the impact of cross-border personal data transfers includes the following contents:

a) Information and contact details of the transferor of personal data, the recipient of personal data, the processor of personal data and other parties involved in the cross-border transfer of personal data;

b) Contact details of personal data protection department and personnel; Organizations and individuals providing personal data protection services (if any) of the party transferring personal data and receiving personal data;

c) Describe and explain the purpose of cross-border personal data transfer, types of cross-border personal data transfer, details of cross-border personal data transfer and processing activities, and personal data processing flow diagram;
d) Description and explanation of the implementation of obtaining consent from personal data subjects, policies on storing, deleting and destroying personal data;
d) Plan to ensure personal data security after cross-border transfer, personal data protection measures, personal data protection standards applied;
e) System diagram, describing features of the personal data storage and processing system after receiving cross-border personal data;
g) Procedures for the recipient of cross-border personal data to transfer and provide personal data to third parties;
h) Results of self-assessment of compliance with regulations on personal data protection of agencies, organizations and individuals with cross-border personal data transfer activities;

i) Assess the level of personal data protection of the recipient of personal data; the impact and risks of cross-border transfer and processing of personal data; Unwanted consequences and damages are likely to occur, and measures to reduce or eliminate those risks.

4. Records assessing the impact of cross-border transfers of personal data must always be available to serve the inspection and assessment activities of the agency responsible for protecting personal data.

The party transferring personal data across borders submits 01 original copy of the complete dossier online or in person or via postal service to the agency in charge of protecting personal data with Form No. 01a/01b in the Appendix to this Decree within 60 days from the date of cross-border personal data transfer.

5. The agency in charge of personal data protection evaluates and returns results for the cross-border personal data transfer impact assessment dossier that meets or fails to meet the requirements within 15 days.

6. The agency in charge of personal data protection evaluates and requests the party transferring cross-border personal data to complete the Dossier assessing the impact of cross-border personal data transfer within 30 days in case the dossier is not complete and in accordance with regulations. In case the party transferring personal data does not complete the dossier in accordance with regulations, the agency in charge of personal data protection shall consider applying regulations on sanctioning administrative violations in the field of personal data protection.

7. The party transferring personal data across borders updates and supplements the Dossier assessing the impact of transferring personal data across borders according to the provisions of Article 20 of this Decree.
Article 19. Conditions, order, procedures, and components of documents assessing the impact of processing personal data

1. The personal data controller, the personal data controller and processor, and the personal data processor shall establish and maintain a Record assessing the impact of processing of their personal data from the time the processing of personal data begins.
2. Documents assessing the impact of processing personal data by personal data controllers, personal data controllers and processors, and personal data processors include:
a) Report on impact assessment of personal data processing according to Form No. 10 in the Appendix to this Decree;
b) Copy of contract or agreement on personal data processing, showing the binding and responsibilities between organizations and individuals in personal data processing activities;
c) Policies, procedures, regulations, forms and other relevant documents on personal data protection of the personal data controller, personal data controller and processor, personal data processor.
3. Report assessing the impact of personal data processing includes the following contents:
a) Information and contact details of the personal data controller, personal data controller and processor, personal data processor, third party;

b) Contact details of personal data protection department and personnel; organizations and individuals providing personal data protection services (if any) of the personal data controller, personal data controller and processor, personal data processor, third party;
c) Description and explanation of the purpose of processing personal data, types of personal data processed, details of personal data processing activities and personal data flow diagram;
d) Description and explanation of the implementation of obtaining consent from personal data subjects, policies on storing, deleting and destroying personal data;
d) Plan to ensure personal data security, personal data protection measures, system design diagram, applicable personal data protection standards;
e) Results of assessment of compliance with regulations on personal data protection;
g) Assess the impact and risks of personal data processing activities; Unwanted consequences and damages are likely to occur, and measures to reduce or eliminate those risks.

4. Documents assessing the impact of processing personal data must always be available to serve the inspection and evaluation activities of the Agency in charge of protecting personal data and submit 01 original copy online or in person or via postal service to the agency in charge of protecting personal data attached with Form No. 02a/02b in the Appendix to this Decree within 60 days from the date of processing personal data.

5. The agency in charge of personal data protection evaluates and returns results for the personal data processing impact assessment dossier that meets or does not meet the requirements within 15 days.

6. The agency in charge of personal data protection evaluates and requests the personal data controller, the personal data controller and processor, and the personal data processor to complete the personal data processing impact assessment dossier within 30 days in case the dossier is not complete and in accordance with regulations. In case the personal data controller, personal data controller and processor, or personal data processor does not complete the dossier in accordance with regulations, the agency in charge of personal data protection shall consider applying regulations on sanctions for administrative violations in the field of personal data protection.

7. The personal data controller, the personal data controller and processor, and the personal data processor update and supplement the personal data processing impact assessment dossier according to the provisions of Article 20 of this Decree.
Article 20. Update records of impact assessment of personal data processing and records of impact assessment of cross-border transfer of personal data
1. Cross-border personal data transfer records and personal data processing impact assessment records are updated periodically every 6 months from the first submission in the following cases:
a) When a new purpose of transferring personal data or a new purpose of processing personal data arises;
b) When the personal data controller, personal data controller and processor, personal data processor, third party arises or changes.
2. Changes that need to be updated immediately within 10 days include:
a) When an agency, organization or unit is reorganized, terminates operations, dissolves or goes bankrupt according to the provisions of law;
b) When there is a change in information about organizations or individuals providing personal data protection services;

c) When there is a change in the industry, occupation, or business service related to personal data processing registered in the personal data processing impact assessment dossier or the impact assessment dossier on cross-border personal data transfer.

3. Updating the personal data processing impact assessment dossier and the impact assessment dossier on cross-border personal data transfer is done according to Form No. 03a/03b in the Appendix to this Decree, submitted online or directly or via postal service to the agency in charge of personal data protection.
Article 21. Personal data processing services
1. Services of providing and operating automated systems and software to process personal data on behalf of the controller, controller and processor.
2. Service of scoring, ranking, and evaluating the creditworthiness of personal data subjects.
3. Online personal data collection and processing service from websites, applications, software and social networks.
4. Services of collecting and processing personal data through websites, applications, health care software, health monitoring, and medical services.

5. Service of collecting and processing personal data through educational applications and software with monitoring elements such as attendance, video recording, behavioral scoring, and emotion recognition.

6. Personal data mining and analysis services, including: using analytical tools to find information, trends and patterns from personal data; Apply data mining methods to extract value from personal data, predict user behavior, or optimize services.
7. Personal data encryption service during transmission and storage.
8. Automatic personal data processing service based on big data technology, artificial intelligence, blockchain, virtual universe.
9. Application platform services provide personal location data.
Article 22. Conditions of organizations providing personal data processing services
1. Is an organization or enterprise established and operating under Vietnamese law, meeting the conditions specified in this Article.
2. Personnel conditions:
a) The head of expertise in personal data processing of the organization is a Vietnamese citizen, permanent resident in Vietnam;
b) Have a management and executive team that meets professional requirements for processing personal data;
c) Have at least 03 qualified personnel as prescribed in Clause 2, Article 13 of this Decree.
3. Have infrastructure, equipment, facilities and technology suitable for personal data processing services.
4. Have satisfactory results for personal data processing impact assessment dossiers and cross-border personal data transfer impact assessment dossiers in case of cross-border personal data transfer.
Article 23. Responsibilities of organizations providing personal data processing services
1. Fully comply with legal regulations on personal data protection; Responsibilities and obligations of personal data controllers and processors and personal data processors.
2. Develop a risk management framework for personal data protection appropriate to the services provided.
3. Conduct an assessment of the current compliance status and credibility of personal data protection periodically once a year.
4. Apply standards and regulations related to data security, personal data protection, and network security.
5. Develop regulations on the responsibilities and powers of organizations in processing personal data.

6. Ensuring the processing of personal data for the right purpose, limiting collection, transfer, and storage appropriately and according to legal regulations; prevent unauthorized access, collection, use, disclosure or similar risks in the processing of personal data.

7. In case of being a personal data processor, the organization requires the personal data controller to obtain the consent of the data subject according to regulations before providing services, ensuring that the personal data subject is informed about the type of personal data processed, the purpose of processing and the organization providing the personal data processing service.
8. Organizations providing personal data processing services shall authenticate the organization's identity in accordance with the law on electronic identification and authentication.
Article 24. Authority to issue, re-issue, replace, and revoke Certificate of eligibility for business of personal data processing services
1. The Ministry of Public Security issues, reissues, renews, and revokes Certificates of eligibility to provide personal data processing services.

2. The Minister of Public Security assigns responsibility to the agency in charge of personal data protection to issue, reissue, renew, and revoke Certificates of eligibility to provide personal data processing services according to regulations.
Article 25. Documents, order and procedures for issuance of Certificate of eligibility for business of personal data processing services
1. Application dossier for issuance of Certificate of eligibility for personal data processing service business includes:
a) Application for issuance of Certificate of eligibility for personal data processing service business according to Form No. 04 in the Appendix to this Decree;
b) Copy of Business Registration Certificate;
c) Document designating the personal data protection department or contract to use personal data protection services according to regulations;
d) Proposal for issuance of Certificate of eligibility for business of personal data processing services;
d) Degrees and other proof documents of personnel specified in Point c, Clause 2, Article 22 of this Decree;
e) Organizations do not have to submit the documents specified in Point b of this Clause in case state agencies can exploit the database.
2. Proposal for issuance of Certificate of eligibility for business of personal data processing services includes the following contents:
a) Necessity and goals;
b) Content and field requested for approval;
c) Industry, business field, business plan;
d) Expected scale of personal data processing activities;
d) Risk management framework for personal data protection;
e) Plan to periodically evaluate the current compliance status and credibility of personal data protection;
g) Application of standards and regulations related to data security and personal data protection;
h) Plan for using electronic identification and authentication services;
i) Responsibilities and powers of the organization in processing personal data;
k) Qualified personnel according to regulations.

3. The organization submits 01 application dossier for issuance of Certificate of eligibility for business of personal data processing services online or in person or via postal service to the agency in charge of personal data protection.

The agency in charge of personal data protection evaluates the application for a Certificate of eligibility for personal data processing service business as satisfactory or unsatisfactory within 10 days. In case the dossier is not complete and in accordance with regulations, the agency in charge of personal data protection shall notify in writing the organization requesting to supplement and complete the dossier within 15 days and clearly state the reason.

4. Within 30 days from the date of receipt of complete and valid documents, the agency in charge of personal data protection shall appraise, review and decide to issue a Certificate of eligibility for personal data processing service business according to Form No. 05 in the Appendix to this Decree. Certificate of eligibility to provide personal data processing services in paper or electronic form; Paper copies are issued in case of direct application or via postal service or upon request when submitting online application through the public service portal. In case of refusal, the agency responsible for personal data protection shall notify the organization in writing and clearly state the reason.
Article 26. Documents, order and procedures for re-issuance and renewal of Certificate of eligibility for business of personal data processing services
1. Reissue when the paper copy of Certificate of eligibility for personal data processing service business is lost or damaged.

a) In case the organization needs to reissue a paper copy, send an application according to Form No. 05 in the Appendix to this Decree online or directly or via postal service to the agency responsible for protecting personal data.

b) Within 05 working days from the date of receipt of the application according to regulations, the agency in charge of personal data protection shall consider and re-issue the Certificate of eligibility for business of personal data processing services; In case of refusal, there must be a written notice clearly stating the reason.
2. Reissue in case of incorrect information or change in the content of the Certificate of eligibility to provide personal data processing services.
a) Documents include: Application according to Form No. 06 in the Appendix to this Decree; Papers and documents proving incorrect information or changes in information on the issued Certificate.

b) The organization submits 01 set of documents mentioned above online or in person or via postal service to the agency responsible for protecting personal data. Within 05 working days from the date of receipt of complete documents specified in Point a of this Clause, the agency in charge of personal data protection shall consider and decide to renew the Certificate of eligibility for business of personal data processing services; In case of refusal, there must be a written notice clearly stating the reason.
Article 27. Revocation of Certificate of eligibility to provide personal data processing services
1. Revoke the Certificate of eligibility to provide personal data processing services in the following cases:
a) When one of the conditions in Clause 1 and Clause 2, Article 26 of this Decree is not met;
b) Not providing services for 12 months or more;
c) Be dissolved or go bankrupt according to the provisions of law;
d) Failure to remedy violations of personal data protection, information security, network security, and data security as requested by competent state agencies;
d) Proactively request to suspend or terminate operations.

2. The agency in charge of personal data protection shall decide to revoke the Certificate of eligibility for personal data processing service business according to Form No. 07 in the Appendix to this Decree.

3. Organizations providing personal data processing services are responsible for returning the Certificate issued to the Agency in charge of personal data protection within 05 working days from receipt of the revocation decision.

4. The agency in charge of personal data protection shall issue a decision on revocation of the Certificate of eligibility for personal data processing service business and notify on the National Information Portal on personal data protection.
Article 28. Content of notice of violation of regulations on personal data protection
1. Contents of notice of violation of regulations on personal data protection include:
a) Describe the nature of the violation of personal data protection regulations, including: time, location, behavior, organization, individual, types of personal data and quantity of data involved;
b) Contact details of the personal data protection department or personnel or the organization or individual providing personal data protection services;
c) Describe the possible consequences and damages of violating personal data protection regulations;
d) Describe the measures taken to resolve and minimize the harmful effects of violations of personal data protection regulations.

2. The personal data controller, the personal data controller and processor, and the third-party processor shall send a notice of violation of personal data regulations to the agency in charge of personal data protection or via the National Information Portal on personal data protection according to Form No. 08 in the Appendix to this Decree.
Article 29. Notice of breach of personal location data and biometric data
1. When a personal data breach involving location data or biometric data occurs, the personal data controller or the party that controls and processes personal data is responsible for:
a) Notify the affected data subject within no more than 72 hours from the time of discovery of the violation;
b) Report to competent state agencies as prescribed in Article 28 of this Decree;

c) Record, store and update violation records to serve inspection, examination and handling. Organizations must retain violation records for a minimum period of 5 years from the date the incident is resolved.
2. Notification to data subjects according to point a, clause 1 of this Article must include at least the following contents:
a) Time and form of detection of violations;
b) Type of data affected (location, biometrics, or both);
c) The severity and possible risks to the data subject's legitimate rights and interests;
d) Measures that have been and will be taken to fix the problem and minimize damage;
d) Instruct data subjects to take further preventive measures;

e) Contact information of the personal data protection department or personnel or the organization or individual providing personal data protection services; Department that receives and handles personal data incidents at the organization.

3. In case an organization or individual cannot notify all affected personal data subjects within the time limit stated in Clause 1 of this Article due to technical or emergency reasons, the following must be done:
a) Public announcement by official electronic means of the organization via website or application;
b) Send notification to relevant personal data subjects as soon as technical conditions allow.

4. In case organizations or individuals fail to notify on time or intentionally delay or avoid the notification obligation, they will be considered and handled for violations according to the provisions of law.
Article 30. Responsibility for implementing international cooperation on personal data protection
1. The agency responsible for personal data protection is responsible for helping the Ministry of Public Security carry out international cooperation on personal data protection.

2. Ministries, ministerial-level agencies, and Government agencies carry out international cooperation on personal data protection for industries and fields under their management according to the provisions of law and assigned functions and tasks.
3. Provincial People's Committees carry out international cooperation on personal data protection according to the provisions of law and assigned functions and tasks.
Article 31. Checking personal data protection activities
1. Checking personal data protection activities is conducted regularly or unexpectedly in the following cases:
a) When there are grounds to suspect violations of the law on personal data protection;
b) When there is direction from an agency or person with state management authority on personal data protection;
c) Carry out state management work according to the provisions of law.
2. Subjects inspected for personal data protection activities include:
a) Agencies, organizations, and individuals that process personal data;
b) Organizations and individuals providing personal data processing services;
c) Agencies, organizations and individuals must carry out impact assessment of personal data processing and impact assessment of cross-border transfer of personal data;
d) Agencies, organizations, and individuals involved in cases or violations of regulations on personal data protection.
3. Content of checking personal data protection:
a) Current status of compliance with personal data protection;
b) Activities to assess the impact of processing personal data and assess the impact of cross-border transfers of personal data;
c) Personal data processing service business activities.

4. The agency in charge of personal data protection issues an inspection decision and notifies the inspection subjects specified in Clause 2 of this Article 15 days in advance of the time, content and composition of the inspection team. In case of unexpected inspection to promptly verify, detect and prevent violations of the law on personal data protection, the agency in charge of personal data protection has the right to conduct the inspection immediately without prior notice.

5. Inspection subjects must fully prepare appropriate inspection contents according to the provisions of Clause 3 of this Article and according to specific requirements in the inspection decision issued by the agency responsible for personal data protection.
Article 32. Research and develop solutions to protect personal data
Agencies, organizations, businesses and individuals participating in research, development and application of personal data protection solutions, including:
1. Build software systems and equipment to protect personal data;
2. Method for evaluating software and personal data protection equipment to meet standards;
3. Methods to check that the provided hardware and software function properly;
4. Record and manage compliance with regulations on personal data protection;
5. Address the risk of exposure and loss of personal data;
6. Technical initiatives to raise awareness and skills on personal data protection;
7. Processing personal data for statistical and scientific work;

8. Solutions for personal data subjects to control their personal data, provide and share data according to selective disclosure mechanisms, applying high technology and strategic technology in accordance with international standards and regulations;
9. Personal data protection standards;
10. Other personal data protection solutions in accordance with legal regulations.
Article 33. Content of state management of personal data protection
1. Issue according to authority or submit to competent state agencies to promulgate legal documents on personal data protection; Organize the implementation of laws on personal data protection.
2. Develop and organize the implementation of strategies, policies, schemes, projects, programs and plans on personal data protection.
3. Guide agencies, organizations and individuals on measures, processes, standards and regulations to protect personal data according to the provisions of law.
4. Propagate and educate about laws on personal data protection; communicate and disseminate knowledge and skills to protect personal data.
5. Build, train, foster and develop human resources on personal data protection.
6. Inspect, examine, reward, resolve complaints and denunciations and handle violations of the law on personal data protection according to the provisions of law.
7. Statistics, information, and reports on the situation of personal data protection and the implementation of laws on personal data protection to competent state agencies.
8. Organize preliminary and final reviews and scientific research on personal data protection; Research and apply scientific and technological advances in personal data protection.
9. International cooperation on personal data protection.
Article 34. Responsibilities of the Ministry of Public Security
1. Help the Government unify the implementation of state management on personal data protection.

2. Preside over the development, promulgation or submission to competent state agencies to promulgate and guide the implementation of legal documents guiding the implementation of legal regulations on personal data protection.
3. Guide and implement personal data protection activities, protect the rights of data subjects against violations of legal regulations on personal data protection.

4. Preside and coordinate with the Ministry of Science and Technology to develop standards and technical regulations for personal data protection; Technical regulations on de-identification of personal data and anonymization of personal data are issued and applied in Vietnam.
5. Build, manage and operate the National Information Portal on personal data protection.
6. Deploy propaganda, dissemination, legal education and guidance and fostering knowledge and skills on personal data protection for personal data protection forces.
7. Evaluate, summarize and summarize the results of personal data protection work of relevant agencies, organizations and individuals.
8. Promote scientific research on personal data protection; Research and apply scientific and technological advances in personal data protection to innovate in the field of personal data protection.
9. Deploy international cooperation activities on personal data protection.
Article 35. Responsibilities of the Ministry of National Defense

1. Coordinate with the Ministry of Public Security and relevant agencies and organizations to arrange forces and means to protect personal data, detect and prevent violations of regulations on personal data protection within the scope of management.

2. Within the scope of assigned functions and tasks, preside over and coordinate with the Ministry of Public Security to evaluate the results of personal data protection for agencies, organizations and individuals under their management; promote the application of personal data protection measures; Research and apply advanced security technologies in processing and protecting personal data for national defense activities; Carry out international cooperation on personal data protection within the scope of management according to the provisions of law.

3. Organize propaganda, dissemination, and education of laws and knowledge and skills to protect personal data for officers, professional soldiers, officers, civil servants, and public employees within the scope of management.

4. Inspect, examine, supervise and handle violations of regulations on personal data protection for agencies, organizations and individuals under the management of the Ministry of National Defense according to the law and assigned functions and tasks.
Article 36. Responsibilities of the Ministry of Science and Technology

1. Coordinate with the Ministry of Public Security in developing standards and technical regulations for personal data protection; Technical regulations on de-identification of personal data and anonymization of personal data are issued and applied in Vietnam.

2. Coordinate with the Ministry of Public Security and relevant agencies in researching, developing, mastering and applying personal data protection measures applying advanced technology, high technology, strategic technology, forming solutions, products and services to protect personal data in the process of developing digital government, digital economy, digital society through science and technology programs.
Article 37. Responsibilities of ministries, ministerial-level agencies, and agencies under the Government
1. Implement state management of personal data protection for industries and fields under the scope of management according to the provisions of law.
2. Develop and implement the contents and tasks of personal data protection in this Decree.
3. Supplement regulations on personal data protection in developing and implementing tasks of ministries and branches.

4. Arrange personnel and personal data protection departments at units under management; Ensure capacity is met, suitable for job position and professional requirements on personal data protection according to the provisions of law.
5. Arrange funding for personal data protection activities according to current budget management decentralization.
6. Organize propaganda and dissemination of laws, professional training and skills for officials, civil servants, public employees and units under the management scope of personal data protection.
7. Coordinate with the Ministry of Public Security in inspection, examination, supervision, and handling of violations of regulations on personal data protection within the scope of management.

8. Coordinate with the Ministry of Public Security and the Ministry of Science and Technology in developing instructions and implementing technical standards and regulations on personal data protection for units, organizations and individuals under their management.
Article 38. Responsibilities of the People's Committees of provinces and centrally run cities
1. Implement state management of personal data protection for management sectors and fields according to the provisions of law on personal data protection.
2. Implement regulations on personal data protection in this Decree.

3. Arrange personnel and personal data protection departments at units under management; Ensuring that capacity conditions are met, appropriate to the job position and professional requirements on personal data protection according to the provisions of law.
4. Arrange funding for personal data protection activities according to current budget management decentralization.
5. Organize propaganda and dissemination of laws and knowledge and skills to protect personal data for officials, civil servants, public employees, people and businesses in the area.

6. Organize training to improve the capacity of teams working on personal data protection at administrative levels; Integrate personal data protection content in administrative reform and digital transformation programs.

7. Build a statistical system, synthesize and periodically report on the personal data protection situation in the locality to send to the agency in charge of personal data protection according to regulations.
Article 39. Agency in charge of personal data protection and National Information Portal on personal data protection

1. The agency in charge of personal data protection is a unit under the Ministry of Public Security, responsible for helping the Minister of Public Security perform state management functions on personal data protection.

2. National information portal on personal data protection is a place to provide information to propagate the Party's guidelines, guidelines and policies, and the State's laws on personal data protection; Support guidance, raise awareness and personal data protection skills for agencies, organizations and individuals; Receive and process feedback and recommendations from relevant agencies, organizations and individuals; Carry out other activities in accordance with the law on personal data protection.
Article 40. Funding to ensure personal data protection activities

1. Funding for personal data protection includes the state budget; Support from domestic and foreign agencies, organizations and individuals; Revenue from providing personal data protection services; international aid and other legal revenue sources.

2. Funding for personal data protection of state agencies is guaranteed by the state budget, arranged in the annual state budget estimates. The management and use of funds from the state budget is carried out in accordance with the law on state budget.
3. Funds for protecting personal data of organizations and businesses are arranged and implemented by organizations and businesses themselves according to regulations.
Article 41. Regulations on application of Clauses 2 and 3 of Article 38 of the Law on Personal Data Protection

1. Small businesses and startups have the right to choose whether or not to implement the provisions of Article 21, Article 22 and Clause 2, Article 33 of the Law on Personal Data Protection within 5 years from the effective date of the Law on Personal Data Protection, except for small businesses and startups that provide personal data processing services, directly process sensitive personal data or process personal data from the time they reach scale. from 100 thousand personal data subjects or more based on the cumulative results of the total amount of processed personal data.

2. Business households and micro enterprises are not required to comply with the provisions of Article 21, Article 22 and Clause 2, Article 33 of the Law on Personal Data Protection, except for business households and micro enterprises providing personal data processing services, directly processing sensitive personal data or processing personal data from the time their scale reaches 100 thousand personal data subjects or more based on the cumulative result of the total amount of personal data collected. processing.
Article 42. Effect and responsibility for implementation
1. This Decree takes effect from January 1, 2026.
2. Decree No. 13/2023/ND-CP dated April 17, 2023 of the Government on personal data protection expires from the effective date of this Decree.
3. Amend Clause 2, Article 16 of Decree No. 165/2025/ND-CP dated June 30, 2025 of the Government detailing a number of articles and measures to implement the Data Law as follows:
“2. The protection of core data, important data that is personal data, is implemented in accordance with the provisions of the Law on Personal Data Protection and detailed implementing regulations.

In case of transferring and processing cross-border core data and important data that is personal data, the owner of the core data and important data shall carry out a dossier to assess the impact of processing personal data and assess the impact of transferring personal data across borders according to the provisions of law on personal data protection; Do not have to conduct risk assessment, impact assessment of cross-border data transfer and processing according to the provisions of this Decree.”
4. The Ministry of Public Security is responsible for guiding, inspecting and urging the implementation of this Decree.

5. Ministers of ministries, heads of ministerial-level agencies, agencies under the Government, Presidents of People's Committees of provinces and centrally-run cities and relevant agencies, organizations and individuals are responsible for implementing this Decree.
